text: The surgeon identifies the lateral pelvic fascia, revealing the tissue caliber between the prostate capsule and the underlying neurovascular bundle during the initial step of the nerve sparing dissection.
The magnified view reveals nerve fibers within a web of tissue in the lateral pelvic fascia area, highlighting intricate anatomical details.

The surgeon retracts the seminal vesicle on the left side, revealing the pedicle, which serves as the main blood supply to the posterior aspect of the prostate. The pedicle is grasped between the forceps.

The surgeon places an additional clip, revealing the beginning and end of the nerve bundle. A sharp incision is made with minimal blood loss, allowing precise dissection along the prostate capsule in the presence of low volume disease. Another clip is placed to facilitate complete dissection of the nerve bundle.

The surgeon applies minimal traction on the prostate, revealing the triangular-shaped nerve bundle sweeping off to the prostate capsule. The magnified view allows observation of individual nerve fibers at the millimeter level.
The surgeon uses cautery to manage small perforating blood vessels encountered during the dissection near the prostate capsule.
The surgeon performs the right-sided neurovascular dissection, revealing the capsule of the prostate and the base of the prostate positioned above the scissors.

The surgeon carefully instructs the assistant on precise clip placement on individual blood vessels within the pedicle, visible in the surgical field. The nerve dissection on the right side is shown, with the nerve positioned lateral and below the prostate.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to place a clip on the nerve bundle, allowing it to fall laterally and posteriorly. After cutting, the neurovascular bundle begins to peel off smoothly from the prostate capsule, similar to the opposite side.

The assistant places the nerve bundle under slight tension, aiding in visualizing the precise area for dissection. The nerve bundle appears as a tubular structure, approximately one centimeter wide, positioned above the sucker tip and to the right of the current dissection site.

The surgeon incises the dorsal vein complex to establish continuity between the nerve dissection and urethral dissection. The yellow Foley catheter is visible in the midline. The surgeon connects the nerve dissection laterally and apically using the robotic system for precise manipulation in the confined surgical field.

The surgeon carefully sweeps the nerves laterally to avoid damage while cutting the posterior urethral plate. The dissection proceeds through the recto urethralis tissue located behind and underneath the prostate, maintaining precision to ensure nerve sparing.

The prostate is detached, revealing the neurovascular bundles with clips placed on the right and left sides. The surgeon uses a vascular Doppler ultrasound to assess the arterial pulse within these bundles, which is crucial for maintaining blood flow to the cavernosal artery.
